name: Acceptance Criteria
definition: Testable conditions that define when a story is complete, written as a hierarchical list with up to three levels of nesting. Also called ACs. Criteria can have sub-criteria and leaf-level criteria, support attachments, and link to persistent URLs. Any edit to acceptance criteria is versioned and recorded in the story's Activities log. When a story is split, criteria are preserved based on whether their parent criteria move with them.

name: Active Sprint
definition: The single sprint a Scrum team is currently executing. Only one sprint can be active per team at a time. Items in the active sprint appear on the In Progress page and contribute to burndown charts and velocity calculations when the sprint closes. A new sprint cannot be started until the current one is completed or cancelled.

name: Activities
definition: A chronological, filterable log of all changes to a story, bug, epic, or feature flag including title changes, workflow transitions, assignments, comments, attachments, subtasks, and pull requests. Each entry shows who performed the action and when. Activities can be filtered by type or source (Atono, Integration, API, MCP Server).

name: Administrator
definition: A workspace role with full administrative privileges including managing workspace settings, environments, users, integrations, and SSO configuration, but without access to billing. Administrators can assign other Administrators and Product Managers but cannot remove their own role.

name: Assignee
definition: The user responsible for completing a story or bug. Assignment triggers optional email notifications and helps track ownership. Items can be unassigned (No assignee) or reassigned to different users.

name: Attachment
definition: A file added to a story or bug, displayed as thumbnails for images/videos or as file info for other types. Can be attached inline to specific acceptance criteria or to the general attachments area. Limited to 100 files per item, 50 MB per file, with workspace-level storage limits based on plan. Attachment URLs expire after a short time and must be re-fetched.

name: Blocker
definition: An impediment preventing a story or bug from progressing through the workflow. Blockers can be internal (a missing decision, an unresolved dependency) or external (a third-party outage, waiting on another team). Identified in daily standups, blockers should be escalated and resolved by the team or Scrum Master as quickly as possible to minimize cycle time impact.

name: Bug
definition: An error, flaw, or unintended behavior in software that requires investigation and resolution. Each bug includes a title, description (summary, reproduction steps, expected and actual behavior), priority, severity, affected environments, and an optional assignee. Bugs move through team workflows and support threaded comments, subtasks, attachments, and version history. Triage happens before a bug is assigned to a team for active work.

name: Comment
definition: A discussion thread on a story or bug, either on the item as a whole or anchored to specific highlighted text or an acceptance criterion. Supports @mentions (which trigger notifications), inline item ID references, and per-comment actions including accept, reject, edit, and delete. Open comments appear in a side panel; resolved comments are archived in Activities.

name: Customer
definition: A named tenant or organization whose users interact with your product, identified by a string value passed to the Atono SDK or Chrome extension. Used to scope feature flag slices to specific customers and to filter Feature Engagement graphs. Customer values appear in Slice conditions, usage tracking events, and Engagement page filters.

name: Definition of Done
definition: A team-wide agreement specifying all conditions a story or bug must satisfy before it can be marked complete. Criteria typically include code review, automated tests passing, acceptance criteria verified, and documentation updated. The definition of done is applied consistently across all work items and is distinct from acceptance criteria, which are story-specific. Teams can view and edit their definition of done in team settings.

name: Dependency
definition: A relationship where one work item cannot be started or completed until another item is finished. Dependencies create sequencing constraints and are a common source of sprint planning risk. Identifying dependencies early helps teams reorder work, negotiate delivery, or split stories to reduce blocking chains. In Atono, dependencies can be represented using linked items.

name: Done (Workflow Category)
definition: A workflow category containing steps representing completed work. Items in Done steps do not contribute to cycle time. Each workflow must have at least one Done step. In the default workflow, the Done step is in this category.

name: Epic
definition: A planning item that groups related stories under a single shared goal, providing a high-level view of progress across multiple pieces of work. Epics include a title, one or more user stories defining intended outcomes, and a description. Stories can belong to only one epic at a time. When a story is split, both resulting stories remain in the original epic unless a new epic is created during the split. Epics appear in search results, the Everything page, and timelines.

name: Feature Flag
definition: A named toggle that controls whether a feature is active in a given environment without requiring a code deployment. Each flag has a unique key, an optional description, a flag owner, and per-environment on/off states. Flags can target specific customers via slices. They can be created standalone or linked to a story. The Atono SDK evaluates flags at runtime and records evaluation timestamps for observability.

name: Feature Request
definition: A suggestion from a user, customer, or stakeholder for new or improved product functionality. Feature requests are the raw material of product discovery and are evaluated against user needs, strategic goals, and delivery capacity before being accepted into the backlog. In Atono, feature requests are typically captured as stories in Story Refinement, where they are refined, sized, and prioritized before being assigned to a team backlog and sprint.

name: Kanban
definition: A team workflow type focused on continuous flow rather than time-boxed iterations. Kanban teams pull work from a 'To do' queue into active workflow steps, aiming to minimize work in progress and maximize throughput. Estimated completion dates are calculated from average cycle time and backlog queue position. Kanban teams do not have sprints, velocity, or burndown charts.

name: Label
definition: A free-form tag applied to stories, bugs, or epics to support ad-hoc categorization and filtering beyond the built-in fields. Labels are scoped to a workspace and can be created inline when editing a work item. Multiple labels can be applied to a single item. Labels appear in filters and saved views, making it easy to surface cross-team or cross-epic groups of related work.

name: Milestone
definition: A significant checkpoint marking a key delivery or decision point in a product plan, such as a public launch, a regulatory deadline, or a major feature release. In Atono, milestones are represented as release markers on a timeline, providing a visual anchor for planning and communicating delivery commitments.

name: Priority
definition: A relative ranking of work items indicating their order of importance or urgency. In Atono, priority is expressed through backlog ordering: items higher in the backlog queue are expected to be worked on sooner. Teams can also use product themes or labels to signal strategic priority. Backlog owners are responsible for maintaining priority order when backlog management is enabled.

name: Product Goal
definition: A long-term, outcome-oriented objective that provides direction for the product team's work across multiple sprints or releases. The product goal describes a future state of the product that delivers value to users or the business. In Scrum, the product goal is the current strategic objective the product backlog serves. Teams often align product themes and epics with active product goals.

name: Product Owner
definition: The Scrum role responsible for maximizing the value of the product by managing, ordering, and communicating the contents of the product backlog. The product owner is the single accountable person for backlog priority decisions and ensures the team always works on the highest-value items. In Atono, the product owner's responsibilities are typically held by a user with the Backlog Owner team role and the Product Manager workspace role, giving them control over backlog ordering, sprint planning, and product themes.

name: Release
definition: A key delivery milestone represented by a date, created as release markers on timelines. Release names must be unique within a timeline. Releases can be surfaced in team backlogs: Scrum backlogs show releases as horizontal rows between sprints; Kanban backlogs let you select one release to highlight items projected to complete within that window.

name: Risk
definition: A potential future event or condition that could negatively affect the delivery or quality of a story, sprint, or release. Risks differ from issues in that they have not yet occurred. Teams commonly identify and track risks during sprint planning or retrospectives. In Atono, bug risk is quantified as a risk rating (probability × impact), but broader project risks are typically discussed in team meetings or tracked in story descriptions.

name: Roadmap
definition: A strategic visual plan showing the planned sequence and timing of product work across epics, releases, and themes. Roadmaps help teams and stakeholders understand what is being built, in what order, and why. In Atono, high-level roadmapping is supported through product themes, epics, timelines, and timeboxes.

name: Scrum
definition: A team workflow type focused on time-boxed iterations called sprints. Scrum teams plan using story points and velocity, with team pages including Backlog, In Progress, and Sprint Calendar. Supports burndown charts, velocity tracking, and sprint-based planning.

name: Sprint
definition: A time-boxed iteration for Scrum teams, typically one to four weeks, with defined start and end dates and a committed set of stories working toward a sprint goal. Each team can have one active sprint and unlimited future sprints planned. Sprint scope can be adjusted mid-sprint with a warning. Completed sprints contribute to velocity calculations. Sprint history is visible in the Sprint Calendar.

name: Sprint Capacity
definition: The total amount of work, measured in story points, that a team plans to complete in a sprint based on available team members, their working hours, and any planned time off. Sprint capacity is set during sprint planning and compared against the sum of story points in the sprint backlog to avoid overcommitment. A team's historical velocity is the primary input for setting a realistic capacity target.

name: Sprint Goal
definition: A single, concise objective the Scrum team commits to achieving by the end of a sprint, set during sprint planning. The sprint goal provides focus and helps the team prioritize decisions when scope changes mid-sprint. In Atono, the sprint goal is displayed at the top of the active sprint view and is included in sprint review and retrospective records.

name: Sprint Planning
definition: A Scrum ceremony at the start of each sprint where the team selects stories from the backlog and commits to a sprint goal. The team reviews capacity, estimates remaining uncertainty, and agrees on which items can realistically be completed. In Atono, sprint planning is done from the Backlog page and may be restricted to backlog owners when Backlog Management is enabled.

name: Stakeholder
definition: A user with a critical team role (Team Administrator or Backlog Owner) who is not a team member. Displayed in a separate Stakeholders section on the Team Overview page for visibility.

name: Story
definition: The primary unit of user-focused work in Atono. Each story has a title, one or more user stories in 'As a / I want to / So that' format, acceptance criteria, story points, an assignee, an epic, and optional linked feature flags. Stories move through a team's workflow, support comments, attachments, subtasks, and version history, and can be duplicated or split into multiple stories.

name: Story Points
definition: A numerical measure derived from story size (XXS=1, XS=2, S=3, M=5, L=8, XL=13, XXL=21) using Fibonacci numbers. Unsized stories are assigned the nearest Fibonacci number to the team's average. Used for sprint capacity planning, burndown charts, and velocity tracking in Scrum teams.

name: Story Status
definition: The current position of a story within a team's workflow, represented by a workflow step such as To Do, In Progress, In Review, or Done. Story status determines which workflow category the item falls into and affects visibility on boards, burndown charts, and velocity calculations. Status changes are recorded in the Activities log with a timestamp and the name of the user who made the change.

name: Subtask
definition: An implementation step within a story or bug with title, optional description (up to 1000 chars with rich text), assignee, and status (To do, In progress, Done, Won't do). Displayed with a progress meter excluding Won't do items. Changes are logged in Activities with dedicated filter. Can be reordered by dragging. Not available on drafts.

name: Team
definition: A group of users organized to plan and deliver work together in Atono, using either Scrum or Kanban workflow. Each team has its own workflow, backlog, members, 1+ team admins, optional backlog owners, and can be public or private. Limited to 50 teams per workspace, 50 members per team. Has optional time zone setting for consistent reporting.

name: Team Member
definition: A team role granted automatically when a user joins a public or private team. Can edit team name/description, connect/remove Slack channels, and (for private teams) access the team backlog and manage team membership.

name: Timeline
definition: A visual schedule of timeboxes created and owned by a user, showing how work is organized over time. Supports scrolling, zooming (days to 5 years), filtering by team/product theme, release markers, and timebox management (add, reorder, resize, color, remove). Displays warnings for timeboxes ending within 3 months that have delivery risks.

name: To do (Workflow Category)
definition: A workflow category containing one step representing work ready to start but not yet active. Time in To do steps does not contribute to cycle time. Required for all workflows. In the default workflow, the To do step is in this category.

name: Triage
definition: The process of reviewing newly created bugs or issues to assess their severity, priority, and validity before they enter active development. During triage a team member verifies the report, assigns a severity level, sets a priority, and routes the item to the appropriate team or backlog. In Atono, bug triage is supported by severity and priority fields, bulk editing, and filtered views that surface unreviewed items.

name: User Story
definition: A brief, structured description of a feature from an end user's perspective, written in the format: 'As a [persona], I want to [action] so that [benefit].' Each story or epic can contain one or more user stories. The persona identifies the type of user; the action describes the desired capability; the benefit explains the underlying motivation or outcome. User stories guide acceptance criteria authoring and help teams stay focused on user value.

name: Velocity
definition: The average number of story points a Scrum team completes per sprint, calculated from the team's most recent completed sprints within the configured averaging window. Teams with fewer completed sprints than the window use all available data. Velocity informs sprint planning by indicating how much work a team can realistically take on, and is used to project completion dates for backlog items.

name: Won't do (Workflow Category)
definition: A workflow category containing one step for work that will not be completed. Items in Won't do do not appear in some views, don't contribute to cycle time, velocity, or burndown charts, and are excluded from staleness indicators. Each workflow must have exactly one Won't do step.

name: Workflow
definition: A customizable sequence of 4–25 named steps that stories and bugs move through from ready-to-start to completion. Each step belongs to one of four workflow categories: To do, In Progress, Done, or Won't do. Steps have a name and color. Only one step can exist in the To do category and one in the Won't do category; the In Progress and Done categories can have multiple steps. Cycle time is measured across all In Progress steps. Teams can reorder steps and rename them; the workflow is shared across all team members.

name: Workflow Category
definition: One of four organizational groupings for workflow steps: To do (1 step), In Progress (1+ steps, contributes to cycle time), Done (1+ steps), Won't do (1 step). Categories standardize metrics across teams despite workflow customization. Cannot be empty of applicable steps for stories or bugs.
